title: Listening - Part 2
description: You will hear an archaeologist called Helen talking about opportunities for volunteers at a local excavation. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.
instructions: You will hear an archaeologist called Helen talking about opportunities for volunteers at a local excavation. For questions 9–18, complete the sentences with a word or short phrase.

extracts.context: An archaeologist called Helen talks about opportunities for volunteers at a local excavation.
extracts.audio_script: Hello. I’m Helen, and I coordinate volunteers at the Elmbridge Archaeology Project. This summer we are investigating a piece of land beside the river, before new paths are created there. Some local people imagined the land had always been open fields. However, old records showed that it was once occupied by a [9]pottery workshop[/9], where bowls and cooking pots were produced over two hundred years ago. We are not expecting to find complete objects everywhere, but small pieces can still tell us a great deal about the people who lived and worked in the town.||We welcome adult volunteers and students aged sixteen or over. No previous experience is needed, but everyone must attend an introductory session before joining the excavation. Some people expect this to be a history lecture. There is a little background information, but its most important topic is [10]site safety[/10], because volunteers need to understand how to move around trenches and use small tools responsibly. We supply equipment for digging. Volunteers should bring clothes suitable for dusty work, and the item I strongly recommend even on bright days is a [11]waterproof coat[/11], since the site provides very little shelter when rain begins unexpectedly.||During their first morning, new volunteers do not dig deeply or handle delicate material alone. They usually begin by [12]washing finds[/12], gently removing soil from pieces already checked by a supervisor. It is a patient job, but it helps people recognise different materials. The most common objects we have recovered so far are not coins or jewellery, which receive plenty of attention in films. Most discoveries are fragments of [13]roof tiles[/13], showing the kinds of buildings once standing beside the workshop. Anything interesting is labelled carefully and placed in trays for later study.||We also use technology to create an accurate record of the site. One team uses measuring equipment, while another takes images. This year volunteers can learn to operate a [14]digital scanner[/14], producing three-dimensional records of selected objects without damaging them. Work continues on most weekdays, but visitors can see what we are doing at our monthly [15]open day[/15], when staff give short talks and display recent finds in a temporary tent. Children can try simple activities, though they cannot enter the active excavation area.||Our project involves research after the summer as well. Volunteers who complete twelve sessions will be invited to a [16]museum workshop[/16], where specialists explain how objects are identified and stored. At the end of the season, each regular volunteer receives a written [17]participation certificate[/17], which may be helpful for university or employment applications. Finally, the project needs funds for protective storage boxes and display materials. Rather than selling ordinary souvenirs, we are organising a [18]history walk[/18] through the old town in September, ending at the excavation site. Tickets will be available next week, and application forms for volunteers are on the table by the entrance.

dialogue.speaker: helen
dialogue.text: Hello. I’m Helen, and I coordinate volunteers at the Elmbridge Archaeology Project. This summer we are investigating a piece of land beside the river, before new paths are created there. Some local people imagined the land had always been open fields. However, old records showed that it was once occupied by a pottery workshop, where bowls and cooking pots were produced over two hundred years ago. We are not expecting to find complete objects everywhere, but small pieces can still tell us a great deal about the people who lived and worked in the town.||We welcome adult volunteers and students aged sixteen or over. No previous experience is needed, but everyone must attend an introductory session before joining the excavation. Some people expect this to be a history lecture. There is a little background information, but its most important topic is site safety, because volunteers need to understand how to move around trenches and use small tools responsibly. We supply equipment for digging. Volunteers should bring clothes suitable for dusty work, and the item I strongly recommend even on bright days is a waterproof coat, since the site provides very little shelter when rain begins unexpectedly.||During their first morning, new volunteers do not dig deeply or handle delicate material alone. They usually begin by washing finds, gently removing soil from pieces already checked by a supervisor. It is a patient job, but it helps people recognise different materials. The most common objects we have recovered so far are not coins or jewellery, which receive plenty of attention in films. Most discoveries are fragments of roof tiles, showing the kinds of buildings once standing beside the workshop. Anything interesting is labelled carefully and placed in trays for later study.||We also use technology to create an accurate record of the site. One team uses measuring equipment, while another takes images. This year volunteers can learn to operate a digital scanner, producing three-dimensional records of selected objects without damaging them. Work continues on most weekdays, but visitors can see what we are doing at our monthly open day, when staff give short talks and display recent finds in a temporary tent. Children can try simple activities, though they cannot enter the active excavation area.||Our project involves research after the summer as well. Volunteers who complete twelve sessions will be invited to a museum workshop, where specialists explain how objects are identified and stored. At the end of the season, each regular volunteer receives a written participation certificate, which may be helpful for university or employment applications. Finally, the project needs funds for protective storage boxes and display materials. Rather than selling ordinary souvenirs, we are organising a history walk through the old town in September, ending at the excavation site. Tickets will be available next week, and application forms for volunteers are on the table by the entrance.

questions.question: The land being investigated was once used for a (9) .
questions.explanation: Helen explains that records showed the site was once occupied by a 'pottery workshop'.

questions.question: The most important subject in the introduction for volunteers is (10) .
questions.explanation: Helen says the main topic of the introductory session is 'site safety'.

questions.question: Helen particularly recommends that volunteers take a (11) .
questions.explanation: Helen advises volunteers to take a 'waterproof coat' because there is little shelter.

questions.question: A typical first task for new volunteers is (12) .
questions.explanation: Helen says new volunteers usually start by 'washing finds'.

questions.question: The objects discovered most frequently so far are pieces of (13) .
questions.explanation: Helen identifies fragments of 'roof tiles' as the most common objects found.

questions.question: Volunteers can learn to use a (14) to record certain objects.
questions.explanation: Helen explains that volunteers may use a 'digital scanner'.

questions.question: Members of the public can visit the project during its monthly (15) .
questions.explanation: Helen says the excavation can be viewed at a monthly 'open day'.

questions.question: Regular volunteers will be invited to a (16) after the summer.
questions.explanation: Helen announces a 'museum workshop' for people completing twelve sessions.

questions.question: At the end of the season, regular volunteers receive a (17) .
questions.explanation: Helen says regular volunteers receive a 'participation certificate'.

questions.question: Funds will be raised by arranging a (18) in September.
